content.text: An evaluation suite that only runs on demand is not a quality gate — it is a quality measurement. For eval coverage to function as a Definition of Done criterion, specific eval tiers must be mandatory requirements that block feature completion and deployment. The discipline of treating eval results as completion criteria, not optional quality signals, fundamentally changes how teams invest in eval coverage and how they interpret results.
What it is

Eval coverage as a DoD criterion means that a feature is not considered done until specific evaluation suites have been run against it, their results have met defined thresholds, and those results have been reviewed and approved as part of the formal completion process. It is not sufficient for eval to exist or for the feature author to report that eval 'looks good'; the DoD requires specific, documented, threshold-validated eval results as a mandatory artifact.

Practically, this means the DoD specifies: which eval tier(s) must pass (regression, capability, adversarial), what the passing threshold is for each tier (e.g., regression score >= 97%, adversarial: 0 new failures), who reviews the eval results (the feature author, the tech lead, and/or the safety reviewer), and where the results must be stored (linked in the PR, stored in the eval result archive). Each of these specifics must be defined clearly enough that a new team member can apply them without interpretation.
Why it matters

Without eval coverage as a DoD criterion, eval is done when convenient rather than when required. Under delivery pressure, teams defer eval to the next sprint, run a subset of the eval suite and declare success, or accept eval results without reviewing them carefully. By the time a capability reaches production, its actual performance characteristics have not been rigorously measured. The first time the capability is measured against the full eval suite, the results are often different from what the team expected — but by then, it is a production system.

Eval as a DoD criterion also creates a concrete incentive to maintain and expand the eval suite. If engineers know that the eval suite gates their features, they invest in keeping it fast, relevant, and comprehensive. Without this incentive, eval suites accumulate stale scenarios, miss new capability areas, and gradually become less representative of actual usage patterns. DoD-gated eval is the organizational incentive that keeps the eval suite maintained.
Benefits
Guarantees minimum eval coverage for every feature: no capability reaches production without a documented eval result at or above the defined threshold.
Creates incentives to maintain the eval suite: engineers who need to pass the eval to ship have strong incentives to fix stale scenarios and add missing coverage.
Provides a documented performance baseline for every capability: future regressions can be detected against the baseline established at DoD completion.
Forces explicit threshold decisions: teams must decide what 'good enough' looks like numerically, rather than relying on subjective judgment about whether eval results seem acceptable.
Enables longitudinal quality tracking: tracking DoD eval results across releases reveals capability quality trends that are invisible when eval is run ad-hoc.
Creates a safety gate for adversarial eval: by making adversarial eval a DoD requirement, the team ensures safety evaluation happens before every deployment, not reactively.
Trade-offs and limitations
Eval suite quality determines gate quality: if the eval suite has poor coverage, a feature can pass the DoD eval gate while having significant undetected performance gaps.
Threshold calibration is difficult: setting thresholds too high blocks legitimate progress; too low allows poor-quality features through; calibration requires ongoing measurement and adjustment.
Cost of eval at DoD: running eval suites with paid LLM APIs at DoD time adds cost per feature; cost management strategies (tiered suites, prompt caching) are required at scale.
Eval drift: eval scenarios written at the time of initial DoD may not remain relevant as the capability evolves, creating a gap between what the eval measures and what the feature actually does.
Latency in feedback loop: eval suite runtimes of 10-30 minutes mean that an engineer cannot get rapid feedback during development; eval becomes a final gate rather than a development aid.
How it works

The DoD specifies four eval tiers with different purposes and applicability. Tier 1 (regression suite) runs on every PR as a CI required status check. Tier 2 (task-specific capability eval) runs once per feature, must be completed and approved before the feature is considered done, and its results are stored in the eval archive with a link in the PR. Tier 3 (adversarial/safety eval) runs on every PR that modifies prompts or tool definitions, and is also required at DoD time for any new capability. Tier 4 (full production eval) runs weekly on a schedule and is not a DoD gate but provides the authoritative baseline measurement used to set DoD thresholds.

The DoD checklist item for eval coverage reads: 'Capability eval (Tier 2) results link provided and meet the threshold defined in the eval spec for this capability type. Adversarial eval (Tier 3) results show 0 new failures compared to the current production baseline.' The tech lead verifies these items by clicking the provided links, checking the pass rate against the defined threshold, and reviewing the adversarial eval report for any new failure categories. If the tech lead has questions about an eval result (such as whether a borderline pass is genuinely acceptable), they consult the safety reviewer before marking the item complete.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Four eval tiers: regression (CI gate), capability eval (DoD gate), adversarial eval (DoD gate), full production eval (weekly baseline)

content.text: The diagram shows the four evaluation tiers and their relationship to the DoD. Tiers 1-3 are mandatory gates at different stages of the development and delivery process. Tier 4 provides the full measurement baseline but is not a blocking gate. The tier structure balances cost and speed constraints with coverage requirements.
When to use it

Eval coverage as a DoD criterion applies to any agent capability that will serve real user traffic in production. The specific tier requirements may be adjusted based on risk: a new agent type serving financial decisions requires Tiers 1-3 at DoD; a minor UI improvement to an existing capability may only require Tier 1 (CI regression). The DoD should include a risk classification that determines which eval tiers are required for each change type.

Organizations in early-stage development with small eval suites should start by making Tier 1 regression gating mandatory (even if the regression suite has only 20 scenarios) and adding Tier 2 as a best-effort DoD requirement. As the eval suite matures, Tier 2 and Tier 3 gates become mandatory. Beginning with partial gating builds the team's eval discipline without blocking delivery due to immature coverage.
How to implement
Define the four eval tiers explicitly in documentation: what each tier covers, the scenario count target, the threshold, and the runtime target.
Implement Tier 1 as a required CI status check on all agent PRs; this establishes the baseline CI eval culture before adding DoD requirements.
Create a DoD checklist item for Tier 2 that requires a link to the eval result archive entry; make the archive entry format standardized so tech leads can verify results quickly.
Define per-capability-type thresholds in a configuration file (eval_thresholds.yaml) so that different capability types have documented passing criteria.
Implement Tier 3 adversarial eval as a separate CI workflow triggered on prompt/tool changes, required at DoD time with a zero-new-failures threshold.
Set up the eval result archive (a structured storage location where eval runs are saved with metadata: timestamp, scenario count, pass rate, diff from baseline) so DoD results are traceable.
Establish a threshold review process: revisit thresholds quarterly or after each major capability change to ensure they reflect current capability performance expectations.
Train tech leads on how to interpret eval results, specifically how to identify when a borderline pass warrants concern even if it technically meets the threshold.
Examples

# eval_thresholds.yaml — per-capability DoD thresholds
default:
  regression_suite_pass_rate: 0.97
  adversarial_new_failures: 0

capability_types:
  document_summarization:
    tier2_scenarios: 80
    tier2_pass_rate: 0.92  # lower threshold - task has higher natural variability
    required_evals: [rouge_score, factual_accuracy, length_compliance]

  customer_service_agent:
    tier2_scenarios: 120
    tier2_pass_rate: 0.95
    required_evals: [intent_resolution, tone_compliance, escalation_appropriateness]
    adversarial_scenarios: 40  # higher risk - interacts with customers directly

  data_analysis_agent:
    tier2_scenarios: 60
    tier2_pass_rate: 0.98  # higher threshold - outputs drive business decisions
    required_evals: [numerical_accuracy, citation_present, uncertainty_expressed]

# scripts/run_capability_eval.py — Tier 2 eval runner
import json
import argparse
import hashlib
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path

def run_eval(capability_type: str, prompt_path: str, threshold_config: dict) -> dict:
    """Run Tier 2 capability eval and return structured results."""
    config = threshold_config.get(capability_type, threshold_config['default'])
    # Load eval scenarios for this capability type
    scenarios_path = Path(f'evals/capabilities/{capability_type}/scenarios.jsonl')
    scenarios = [json.loads(l) for l in scenarios_path.read_text().splitlines()]

    results = []
    for scenario in scenarios:
        output = call_model(prompt_path, scenario['input'])
        passed = evaluate_scenario(output, scenario['expected'], scenario['eval_type'])
        results.append({'scenario_id': scenario['id'], 'passed': passed})

    total = len(results)
    passed_count = sum(1 for r in results if r['passed'])
    pass_rate = passed_count / total if total > 0 else 0.0
    threshold = config['tier2_pass_rate']
    met = pass_rate >= threshold

    # Store in archive
    run_id = hashlib.sha256(f'{capability_type}{datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()}'.encode()).hexdigest()[:12]
    archive_entry = {
        'run_id': run_id,
        'timestamp': datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        'capability_type': capability_type,
        'prompt_path': prompt_path,
        'total_scenarios': total,
        'passed': passed_count,
        'pass_rate': pass_rate,
        'threshold': threshold,
        'threshold_met': met,
        'results': results,
    }
    archive_path = Path(f'eval_archive/{run_id}.json')
    archive_path.parent.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
    archive_path.write_text(json.dumps(archive_entry, indent=2))
    print(f'Eval run {run_id}: {pass_rate:.1%} ({passed_count}/{total}) — threshold {threshold:.0%} {"PASS" if met else "FAIL"}')
    print(f'Archive: {archive_path}')
    return archive_entry

def call_model(prompt_path: str, user_input: str) -> str:
    raise NotImplementedError('Implement with your model client')

def evaluate_scenario(output: str, expected: dict, eval_type: str) -> bool:
    raise NotImplementedError('Implement per eval_type')
Real-world example

A legal technology company built an agent for contract review that extracted key terms and flagged risk clauses. During development, the team ran informal eval by testing against five sample contracts and reviewing the outputs manually. The agent shipped to production. Two weeks later, a customer reported that the agent was missing certain clause types in contracts formatted in a particular way. Investigation revealed the issue affected 12% of production contracts — a failure rate that would have been caught by a properly sized eval suite but was invisible with a five-sample informal test.

After the incident, the team implemented DoD-gated eval with a 120-scenario Tier 2 suite covering six contract types and 20 clause categories. Any new capability or prompt change had to pass this suite at 95% before deployment. In the following year, the suite caught four regressions before they reached production, including one that would have caused the agent to fail silently on contracts using a specific document structure introduced by a major software vendor update. The team credited the DoD eval gate with preventing three additional customer-facing incidents.
Common mistakes
Setting thresholds based on what the current capability achieves rather than what the business needs, locking in current quality as the minimum rather than setting a quality target.
Using the same eval scenarios for DoD testing and development iteration, so the model effectively 'trains' to the eval set and the DoD gate provides weaker signal than expected.
Defining eval coverage in terms of scenario count rather than coverage of the capability's actual input distribution, allowing a large but unrepresentative scenario set to provide false confidence.
Not separating adversarial eval results from functional eval results in reporting, making it harder to assess safety regression risk independently from functional regression risk.
Treating a borderline threshold pass (e.g., 97.1% when the threshold is 97%) as a clear approval without investigating the failing scenarios to understand if they represent a systematic gap.
Allowing eval results to expire: a DoD eval run completed two weeks before merge may no longer reflect the current state of the prompt if there have been intervening changes.
Best practices
Maintain eval scenario diversity: ensure scenarios cover the full range of input types, document structures, languages, and edge cases that the capability will encounter in production.
Treat failing eval scenarios as first-class bugs: assign owners, track resolution, and never silently delete failing scenarios to improve the pass rate without addressing the underlying issue.
Decouple eval scenario sets used for development iteration from DoD gate scenarios; the DoD set should be fixed at the time of capability definition and only updated via a formal review process.
Store eval baselines in version control alongside the prompts so that when a prompt changes, the baseline it is measured against is explicit and auditable.
Report eval results in absolute numbers (X of Y scenarios passing) as well as pass rates, so reviewers can assess the magnitude of failures rather than just their percentage.
Include a 'new scenario added' count in DoD eval reports: if the capability adds new behavior, there should be new eval scenarios for that behavior, not just re-running old scenarios against it.
Performance and scalability

Eval suite runtime is the primary scalability constraint. A 150-scenario Tier 2 suite with 3-second API calls per scenario takes 7.5 minutes minimum; with parallelism across 10 workers, this reduces to under 1 minute. Most teams parallelize eval execution from the start: each scenario is independent, so eval is embarrassingly parallel and scales linearly with worker count. The cost constraint is a greater concern than the time constraint at large scenario counts; API cost per DoD eval run should be tracked and budgeted as an explicit engineering cost.

At scale (20+ capabilities, each with their own eval suite), the aggregate CI eval cost can become significant. Teams address this with prompt caching (reusing model responses for scenarios that test unchanged prompt sections), tiered eval environments (a cheaper model tier for CI gating and the production model for DoD verification), and scenario sampling (running a random 50% of scenarios on each PR and the full set only at DoD time). The tradeoff between coverage and cost must be managed explicitly rather than left to each team to address ad-hoc.

Security and reliability

The adversarial eval tier is the primary security-relevant component of DoD eval coverage. By requiring adversarial scenarios to pass at a zero-new-failures threshold, the DoD ensures that every capability and every prompt change is tested against known prompt injection patterns, jailbreak attempts, and safety boundary violations before deployment. This does not provide complete security coverage — adversarial techniques evolve and the eval suite cannot anticipate novel attacks — but it provides a documented minimum safety evaluation that creates accountability and catches known failure modes.

For reliability, the DoD eval gate's most important function is catching regressions before they reach production. By establishing a documented baseline (the Tier 1 regression suite score at the time of last DoD completion) and requiring new features to meet or exceed that baseline, the DoD prevents the gradual degradation of capability quality that occurs when minor regressions slip through one at a time. Each DoD eval gate that passes advances the baseline forward; each gate that requires a revision captures the regression and prevents its accumulation.
Implementation considerations and edge cases

Implementation details vary significantly across organizations based on team size, maturity of the AI platform, and regulatory context. Smaller teams may implement a subset of the practices described here, starting with the highest-impact items and adding others as the team grows. Larger organizations with multiple agent teams may centralize some of these practices in a platform team while distributing others to individual product teams. The key principle is that the practices should match the actual risk profile and operational maturity of the specific system, not be adopted wholesale from a template without consideration of what is genuinely needed.

Edge cases in implementation include: how to handle changes that span multiple categories simultaneously (a prompt change that also modifies tool schemas and adds a new action capability should be reviewed against all relevant criteria, not just the dominant category); how to manage changes made by external contributors or contractors who are not familiar with internal review processes (they require additional onboarding and may need a more senior internal engineer as a sponsor); and how to handle emergency changes during active incidents when the full process cannot be followed (define the emergency procedure before the incident, not during it).

The practices described here are grounded in the current state of agentic AI development as of mid-2025. As the field matures, new tooling will automate more of what is currently a manual process, eval methodologies will improve, and new failure modes will emerge that these practices do not yet address. Teams should treat their engineering process as a living system that evolves with their understanding of agent systems, not as a fixed set of procedures established once and maintained indefinitely without revision.
Eval as a shared team investment

Eval suites are most effective when they are treated as a shared team investment rather than the responsibility of individual feature engineers. When each engineer writes eval scenarios only for their own features, the suite tends to be large in absolute count but thin in cross-cutting coverage. Scenarios added for Feature A and Feature B may individually be high quality but together miss the interaction effects between A and B. A team that periodically dedicates sprint capacity to eval suite maintenance — reviewing scenarios for staleness, adding cross-cutting coverage, and improving scenario difficulty distribution — builds a more robust eval infrastructure than one that only adds scenarios at feature DoD time.

Designating an eval steward role (rotating quarterly among senior engineers) gives someone explicit accountability for the health of the eval suite. The steward's responsibilities include: reviewing proposed new scenarios before they are added to the suite, removing or updating scenarios that have become stale, identifying coverage gaps by analyzing which input types are underrepresented, and reporting on eval suite health metrics (scenario count by category, pass rate trends over time, false-positive rate for adversarial scenarios) at quarterly engineering reviews.

Investing in eval infrastructure — a scenario authoring tool, a result visualization dashboard, a scenario coverage analysis report — pays for itself by reducing the per-scenario cost of adding new coverage. When adding a scenario requires editing a JSONL file and running a validation script, engineers add scenarios freely; when it requires configuring a complex evaluation framework from scratch each time, they add scenarios reluctantly. The platform team should prioritize eval tooling as infrastructure with the same priority as testing infrastructure, not as a nice-to-have that individual teams build ad-hoc.
Communicating eval results to non-technical stakeholders

Eval results that are only legible to engineers cannot serve as the basis for business decisions about deployment risk. As AI capabilities are used to make or support higher-stakes decisions, product managers, legal teams, and executives become stakeholders in the question 'is this capability ready to deploy?' — and they need eval results communicated in terms they can evaluate. A 97.2% regression pass rate is not meaningful to a product manager without context about what the 2.8% failure rate means in terms of user impact.

Effective eval communication for non-technical stakeholders translates pass rates into impact metrics: 'Of 120 test scenarios representing typical customer queries, the agent answered correctly in 114 cases. The 6 failures were in the subcategory of queries about policy changes from 2023, which affects approximately 3% of our customer support volume. We are adding additional coverage for these queries in the next sprint.' This framing gives stakeholders the information they need to make an informed deployment decision rather than asking them to interpret statistical thresholds without context. It also demonstrates that the team has investigated the failure cases rather than simply declaring the threshold met.
Key takeaways
Eval coverage as a DoD criterion means specific eval tiers must be mandatory completion requirements, not optional quality signals that are run when convenient.
Four tiers serve different purposes: CI regression gating, capability-specific DoD gating, adversarial safety gating, and scheduled full-coverage measurement.
Threshold calibration is as important as the gate itself; thresholds must reflect business quality requirements, not just current capability performance.
A good eval suite is maintained alongside the capability it covers: scenarios are updated when the capability evolves, and new scenarios are added for new behavior.
Adversarial eval (Tier 3) must be required at DoD, not optional — it is the only systematic safety evaluation that happens before every deployment.
Store eval results in a structured archive so DoD completion evidence is traceable and baselines are auditable over time.
